meta_title: What size should an outdoor cinema screen be?
meta_desc: From 50 to over 1,500 spectators: a guide to inflatable screen sizes, the viewing distance rule and our tips for choosing.
hero_title: What screen size should you choose for your open-air cinema?
hero_desc: The right size depends primarily on the number of spectators and the available space on your site. Here is the reference table we use to advise our customers — the same one that powers our configurator.
table_desc: Matching your audience size to the width of the projection screen, taking into account the actual footprint of the inflated screen. 2026 specifications for our ranges.
table_note: These are guidelines for an audience seated outdoors. The recommended back row follows the ‘6-screen-width’ rule, as detailed below.
rules_title: Three simple rules to get it right
r1_title: The back row at 6 screen widths
r1_desc: Rule of thumb: the furthest viewer should not be more than six times the width of the image away. An 8-metre canvas remains comfortable from a distance of up to around 48 metres.
r2_title: The first row is 1.5 times the width
r2_desc: If the screen is too close, it strains your neck: make sure the front row is at least one and a half times the width of the screen away.
r3_title: The space above the image matters
r3_desc: The bottom of the screen is positioned between 0.5 m and 3 m above the ground, depending on the model: the larger the audience, the higher the screen needs to be so that people can see over their heads. At drive-in cinemas, the screen is raised to 3 m to allow rows of cars to pass underneath.
l1_desc: Find your exact size in 30 seconds: choose your target audience and intended use, and we’ll recommend the right model for you.
l2_desc: What determines the price of an inflatable screen, and a free, accurate quote within 24 hours.
l3_desc: For a one-off event, hiring is a better option than buying. Guide prices by size.
l4_desc: Airtight or blower, with canvases ranging from 2 m to 22 m: a complete guide to our screens.
q1: What screen size is suitable for 100 viewers?
a1: For an audience of 50 to 150 people, a 6-metre-wide screen is a good place to start. This is the standard size for neighbourhood screenings, campsites and hotels.
q2: What about groups of 500 or more?
a2: For audiences of 500 to 1,500 people, aim for a screen measuring around 12 metres. For larger audiences, our giant screens can reach up to 22 metres in size — ideal for festivals and large open spaces.
q3: How can I check if my plot is big enough?
a3: Measure the depth between the front row (1.5 screen widths) and the back row (6 screen widths), plus the overall dimensions of the inflated screen — for example, 8.20 m wide overall for an 8 m screen — and a technical area for ballast and the blower.
q4: And indoors — what ceiling height do you need?
a4: Check that the ceiling height exceeds the overall height of the inflated screen: from 1.86 m to 7.53 m depending on the model in the airtight range, which is best suited for indoor use as it operates silently without a permanent blower.
cta_title: We’ll help you find the right size — without trying to upsell you
cta_desc: Tell us about your venue and your audience: we’ll recommend the right format and provide a free quote for your project within 24 hours.
